title: Gravitational Potential Energy
summary: Energy stored by lifting. Build PE_g = mgh from scratch, see why only vertical height counts, why the zero line is your choice, and turn stored height into speed — even when friction takes a cut.

steps.title: Energy you store by lifting
steps.body:
Here's a quiet little fact: just by *raising* something, you load it with energy — and gravity is standing by to hand it all back the instant you let go.

Look around and you'll spot it everywhere:

• A **raised hammer**, cocked back before the swing.
• **Water** held high behind a **dam**, ready to spin turbines.
• A **roller coaster** parked at the very top of the first hill, about to plunge.

None of them are moving yet — but each is loaded. We call this stored, position-based energy **gravitational potential energy**. The recurring theme of this lesson: the **higher** you lift it and the **heavier** it is, the more you store.

steps.title: Putting a number on it
steps.body:
Three things set how much gravitational energy you've stored:

• **m** — the object's mass in kilograms (kg). Heavier stores more.
• **g** — gravity's pull, **9.8 m/s²** here on Earth.
• **h** — the height above whatever line you call *zero*, in meters (m).

Multiply all three and you get the stored energy in **joules (J)**. We'll write it as **PE_g** — heads up that many textbooks write the very same quantity as **U** instead.

Play with the explorer below: **lift** the block to change its height and **slide** its mass around. Watch the stored-energy bar climb with *both* — then try to hit the goal.

steps.prompt: A **2 kg** box on Earth (g = 9.8 m/s²). Drag the **height** and watch its stored gravitational energy respond.
steps.task: Drag the height to **double** it — from 1.5 m to 3 m. Then compare the two bars.
steps.solution:
Gravitational PE is PE_g = mgh. Notice height appears just **once** — it isn't squared — so PE_g is *directly proportional* to height. Doubling the height from 1.5 m to 3 m doubles the stored energy, from 29.4 J to **58.8 J (2×)**.

This is the big contrast with kinetic energy (½mv²), where doubling the speed *quadruples* the energy. Height is gentle and linear; speed is not.

steps.prompt: Warm-up. You climb a staircase carrying a **12 kg** backpack, rising **5.0 m** from the floor. Relative to the floor, how much gravitational PE is now stored in the pack? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
hints: Stored gravitational energy is just mass times gravity times height — plug straight into PE_g = mgh.
steps.solution:
Stored gravitational energy is PE_g = mgh, measured here from the floor as the zero line.

PE_g = 12 × 9.8 × 5.0 = **588 J**.

steps.title: Height on a ramp
steps.body:
Objects rarely fall straight down — they **slide along ramps**. So which distance goes into PE_g: the long slope, or the vertical rise?

Only the **vertical** height counts. Gravity pulls straight down, so it only "sees" how far up or down you've actually moved — not how far you traveled along the incline.

For a ramp of length **d** tilted at angle **θ**, basic trig gives the vertical height: **h = d·sin θ**. That single relationship is the bridge between every ramp problem and energy. Drag the critter up and down the slope below — watch the distance **d** it travels and the vertical height **h** it really gains grow together.

steps.prompt:
**Setup:** A **2.0 kg** block is released from rest at the top of a **frictionless** ramp. The ramp is **4.0 m** long along the slope and tilted at **30°**.

**Part A —** How far does the block drop *vertically* by the time it reaches the bottom? (sin 30° = 0.5)
hints: Only the vertical drop matters for PE — convert the slope length using the angle.
steps.solution:
The vertical drop is the ramp length times the sine of the angle: h = d·sin θ.

h = 4.0 × sin 30° = 4.0 × 0.5 = **2.0 m**. (The block travels 4.0 m along the slope, but only drops 2.0 m straight down — and that's all gravity cares about.)

steps.prompt: **Part B —** Over that 2.0 m vertical drop, how much gravitational PE does the **2.0 kg** block lose? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
hints: Use the vertical drop you found in Part A — not the 4.0 m along the ramp.
steps.solution:
The change in stored energy depends only on the vertical drop: ΔPE_g = mg·Δh, using Δh = 2.0 m.

ΔPE_g = 2.0 × 9.8 × 2.0 = **39.2 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part C —** With **no friction**, every joule of lost PE turns into motion. How fast is the **2.0 kg** block moving at the bottom of the ramp?
hints: Frictionless means all 39.2 J of lost PE becomes kinetic energy: ½mv² = ΔPE_g.
steps.solution:
With no friction, the lost gravitational PE becomes kinetic energy: ½mv² = ΔPE_g.

v = √(2·ΔPE_g / m) = √(2·39.2 / 2.0) = √39.2 ≈ **6.26 m/s**.

A beautiful twist: since ΔPE_g = mgh and ½mv² both carry the mass, it cancels — v = √(2gh). The speed at the bottom depends only on the **height dropped**, never on the mass *or* the ramp's angle.

steps.title: Zero is your choice
steps.body:
Here's a subtlety that trips people up: **the PE_g number by itself isn't physical.** *You* decide where h = 0.

Set the zero line at a book on a desk and the book has PE_g = 0. Set the zero line on the floor instead and the *same* book now has a positive PE_g. Same book, two different numbers — and neither is "wrong."

So what *is* real? The **change**, ΔPE_g, as the object actually moves. And that only depends on **Δh** — the height it rises or falls — never on where you happened to call zero.

The practical takeaway: pick whatever zero line makes the math easiest (usually the lowest point of the motion). The physics comes out identical.

steps.prompt: A **1.5 kg** book sits on a desk. You redefine your zero line to be the **desktop itself**, so the book rests right at h = 0. Drag the bar to the book's gravitational PE_g in this reference frame.
hints: Height is always measured from *your* zero line — and you just put the zero line right at the book.
If the book sits exactly at h = 0, what is mgh?
steps.solution:
Height is always measured from the zero line you choose. Here the desktop *is* the zero line, so the book sits at h = 0.

PE_g = mgh = 1.5 × 9.8 × 0 = **0 J**.

The book never moved — only the number did, because we moved the zero line. Any real ΔPE_g between two actual heights would come out exactly the same no matter where we set h = 0.

steps.title: The great trade: PE ⇄ KE
steps.body:
Stored height doesn't just sit there forever — let go, and gravity **trades it for speed**.

As an object falls or slides downhill, its height shrinks, so its gravitational PE drops. With no friction, that energy doesn't vanish — it pours straight into **kinetic energy**, and the object speeds up. Climb back up and the trade runs in reverse: speed converts back into stored height.

This back-and-forth is the engine behind roller coasters, pendulums, and skateboard ramps. Drag the ball up and down the hill below and watch the two bars seesaw: whatever PE gives up, KE gains — the total stays put.

steps.prompt: A **1.5 kg** cart is released from rest at the top of a smooth, **frictionless** track and drops a vertical height of **1.8 m** to the bottom. How fast is it moving at the bottom? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
hints: Frictionless: all the lost PE becomes KE, so mgh = ½mv². Watch what happens to the mass on both sides.
steps.solution:
With no friction, lost PE becomes KE: mgh = ½mv². The mass sits on both sides and cancels, giving v = √(2gh).

v = √(2 × 9.8 × 1.8) = √35.28 ≈ **5.94 m/s**. The 1.5 kg never entered the answer — every object dropped from 1.8 m reaches this same speed.

steps.title: When friction takes a cut
steps.body:
Real ramps aren't perfectly smooth. **Friction** drags backward along the slope, doing **negative** work the whole way down — and that energy leaves the PE-to-KE trade for good (it warms up the surfaces).

So the clean "all PE becomes KE" no longer holds. Instead, the kinetic energy at the bottom is whatever PE was lost **minus** the energy friction skimmed off:

With W_friction = F_f·d (the friction force times the distance along the slope), the object always ends up *slower* than the frictionless case. Up next, you'll put every piece together to find that real speed.

steps.prompt:
**Challenge — put it all together.** A **4.0 kg** crate is released from rest at the top of a **6.0 m** ramp tilted at **30°**. Friction pushes back along the slope with a steady **5.0 N**. (sin 30° = 0.5, g = 9.8 m/s²)

**Part A —** What is the crate's *vertical* drop from top to bottom?
hints: Friction doesn't change the geometry — the vertical drop is still h = d·sin θ.
steps.solution:
The vertical drop comes straight from the ramp geometry: h = d·sin θ.

h = 6.0 × sin 30° = 6.0 × 0.5 = **3.0 m**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** How much gravitational PE does the **4.0 kg** crate lose over that 3.0 m vertical drop? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
hints: Use the vertical drop from Part A — friction doesn't affect how much PE is released by the fall.
steps.solution:
The released gravitational PE depends only on the vertical drop: ΔPE_g = mg·Δh.

ΔPE_g = 4.0 × 9.8 × 3.0 = **117.6 J**. (This is the *total* energy budget the fall provides — friction will claim part of it next.)

steps.prompt: **Part C —** Friction does negative work the whole 6.0 m down the slope, with a force of 5.0 N. Using mgh − W_friction = ½mv², how fast is the **4.0 kg** crate moving at the bottom?
hints: First find the energy friction removes over the full slope length: W_friction = F_f·d. Subtract it from the lost PE to get the leftover KE.
steps.solution:
Friction acts over the full slope distance, not the vertical drop: W_friction = F_f·d = 5.0 × 6.0 = 30 J.

The kinetic energy at the bottom is what's left after friction's cut: ½mv² = mgh − W_friction = 117.6 − 30 = 87.6 J.

Solve for v: v = √(2 × 87.6 / 4.0) = √43.8 ≈ **6.6 m/s**. (Frictionless, the same crate would have reached √(2·9.8·3.0) ≈ 7.7 m/s — friction cost it about a meter per second.)

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You built gravitational PE from the ground up: PE_g = mgh, why only the *vertical* height counts on a ramp, why the zero line is yours to choose while only ΔPE_g is real, and how stored height trades for speed — all the way to a friction-loaded challenge where you tracked every joule from top to bottom.
